section.title: The Call of Isaiah
section.sectionRationale: This is the great commissioning vision at the heart of the book's opening movement: Isaiah sees the LORD high and exalted, is undone by his own uncleanness, is cleansed by a coal from the altar, and volunteers for a mission that will harden rather than immediately heal a stubborn people. It is the theological engine behind everything that follows, judgment delayed by mercy, then finally falling, with only a holy stump remaining.
section.thesis: Isaiah's vision of the thrice-holy LORD exposes his own uncleanness and, once cleansed, sends him to proclaim a message that will deepen the very hardness it exposes, until only a remnant, a holy seed, survives the coming devastation.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the vision of the LORD high and exalted; Isaiah's ruin and cleansing; the commissioning, here am I, send me; and the hard commission to a people who will not turn, ending with the holy seed as a stump.

units.label: The vision of the LORD
units.note: high and exalted, holy, holy, holy

verses.text: In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord seated on a throne, high and exalted; and the train of His robe filled the temple.
Above Him stood {{seraphim|seraphim}}, each having six wings: With two wings they covered their faces, with two they covered their feet, and with two they were flying.

And they were calling out to one another: 
“{{qadosh2|Holy}}, holy, holy is the LORD of Hosts; all the earth is full of His glory.”
At the sound of their voices the doorposts and thresholds shook, and the temple was filled with smoke.

units.label: Ruin and cleansing
units.note: a man of unclean lips touched by a burning coal

verses.text:
Then I said: 
“Woe is me, for I am ruined, because I am a man of unclean lips dwelling among a people of unclean lips; for my eyes have seen the King, the LORD of Hosts.”
Then one of the seraphim flew to me, and in his hand was a glowing coal that he had taken with tongs from the altar.

And with it he touched my mouth and said: 
“Now that this has touched your lips, your {{avon|iniquity}} is removed 
and your sin is atoned for.”

Then I heard the voice of the Lord saying: 
“Whom shall I send? Who will go for Us?” 
And I said: 
“Here am I. Send me!”

units.label: The hard commission
units.note: a hardened people and a holy stump

verses.text:
Make the hearts of this people calloused; deafen their ears and close their eyes. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, 
understand with their hearts, and turn and be healed. ”

Then I asked: 
“How long, O Lord?” 
And He replied: 
“Until the cities lie ruined 
and without inhabitant, 
until the houses are left unoccupied and the land is desolate and ravaged,
until the LORD has driven men far away and the land is utterly forsaken.
And though a tenth remains in the land, it will be burned again. As the terebinth and oak leave stumps when felled, so the {{zera_qadosh|holy seed}} will be a stump in the land.”

seraphim.gloss: Burning ones, from a root meaning to burn. These fiery attendants of God's throne, mentioned only here in the Old Testament by this name, cover their own faces and feet even in God's presence, modeling the appropriate response of reverent creatures before the thrice-holy LORD.

qadosh2.gloss: Holy, set apart, utterly other. The threefold repetition, holy, holy, holy, is the only attribute in Scripture repeated three times for emphasis, the highest possible superlative in Hebrew, and it becomes the defining vision behind Isaiah's favorite title for God, the Holy One of Israel.

avon.gloss: Iniquity, guilt, the twisted bent of sin along with its consequence. The coal from the altar removes Isaiah's avon and atones for his sin, a picture of cleansing that requires contact with the altar, foreshadowing the atonement that would finally deal with guilt fully at the cross.

zera_qadosh.gloss: Holy seed, the tiny remnant surviving even the final felling of the nation, like a stump left after a tree is cut down. This stump image directly anticipates Isaiah 11:1, where a shoot springs from the stump of Jesse, tying the surviving remnant to the coming Messiah.

basic.context: In the year King Uzziah died, a moment of national uncertainty, Isaiah is given an overwhelming vision of the LORD enthroned in the temple, surrounded by seraphim crying holy, holy, holy. Undone by his own sinfulness in the presence of such purity, Isaiah is cleansed by a coal from the altar and then volunteers to be sent, only to receive a hard commission: his preaching will actually harden the people further, until judgment falls and only a small, holy remnant survives like a stump after a tree is felled.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, personal commissioning vision that also functions programmatically for the entire book, explaining why Isaiah's message would be so widely rejected. The most difficult verses are 9-10, where God seems to command hardening rather than repentance; the honest answer, confirmed by how all four Gospels and Acts quote this passage, is that persistent rejection of truth eventually becomes itself a form of judicial hardening, not that God arbitrarily prevents willing repentance.
basic.originalLanguages: The seraphim, burning ones, cover their own faces before the thrice-repeated qadosh, holy, the highest superlative in Hebrew, used nowhere else of any other attribute of God. Isaiah's avon, iniquity, is removed by contact with the altar's coal, and the surviving remnant is called the zera qadosh, the holy seed, a stump image picked up again in Isaiah 11:1 where a shoot springs from the stump of Jesse.
basic.typology: The burning coal from the altar that removes Isaiah's guilt anticipates the full and final atonement accomplished at the cross, where contact with the true altar of sacrifice, Christ Himself, cleanses sin completely. The stump left after felling, the holy seed, directly anticipates the shoot from the stump of Jesse in Isaiah 11:1, the Messiah who springs from a remnant nearly cut off entirely.
basic.christ: John 12:39-41 explicitly says Isaiah saw Christ's glory when he saw the LORD high and exalted, identifying the enthroned figure of Isaiah 6 with Jesus Himself. Jesus quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 to explain why He teaches in parables, Matthew 13:14-15, and the same hardening pattern explains Israel's response to the gospel in Acts 28:26-27 and Romans 11:8, showing that the hardening of Isaiah's day previews and continues into the rejection of Christ.
basic.god: God is holy beyond ordinary human categories, so much so that even sinless angelic beings veil themselves before Him, yet He is also the God who initiates cleansing for a ruined sinner who never could have approached Him on his own. His justice can give a people over to the hardness they have chosen, while His mercy always preserves a holy seed through whom hope continues.
basic.crossReferences: John 12:39-41 identifies the enthroned LORD Isaiah saw as Christ in His glory. Matthew 13:14-15 has Jesus quote this very passage to explain the purpose of His parables. Acts 28:26-27 applies the same hardening text to Israel's rejection of the gospel through Paul. Romans 11:8 draws on this same theme of a spirit of stupor as part of God's larger, ultimately merciful purposes for Israel and the nations.
basic.oneStory: Isaiah 6 shows the pattern that runs through the whole Bible: an encounter with the holy God exposes human sin, but that same God provides cleansing and calls the cleansed into mission, even when that mission will be met with hardened rejection. The holy seed left as a stump carries the story forward to Christ, the shoot who rises from apparent devastation to bring the ultimate atonement Isaiah's coal could only foreshadow.

context.literary: The chapter follows a clear throne-room commissioning pattern found elsewhere in Scripture, Exodus 3, Jeremiah 1, Ezekiel 1-3, Revelation 1, typically involving a vision of God's glory, a sense of human unworthiness, divine cleansing or reassurance, and a specific commission. Its placement after the sweeping indictments of chapters 1-5 explains, only after the fact, both the source of Isaiah's authority and the theological reason for the people's coming unresponsiveness.
context.historical: Dated explicitly to the year King Uzziah died, likely around 740 BC, a moment of political transition and uncertainty for Judah as Assyrian power was rising in the region. The vision inaugurates or formally confirms Isaiah's decades-long prophetic ministry that would span the following reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah.
context.canonical: This is one of the most quoted Old Testament passages in the New Testament specifically regarding hardening and rejection of the gospel, cited by all four Gospel traditions in some form and by Paul in Romans 11, making it a key text for understanding both divine sovereignty and human responsibility in the reception of God's word.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground Isaiah's entire prophetic ministry in a real, overwhelming encounter with God's holiness, and to prepare readers for why the message that follows would be met with such stubborn resistance, explaining the hardening theologically rather than leaving it as a mystery.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a unique, personal commissioning narrative describing Isaiah's specific call; it does not prescribe a required pattern every believer's calling must follow. The enduring truths are that genuine encounter with God's holiness exposes sin, that God graciously cleanses those He calls, and that persistent rejection of truth can itself become a form of judgment.

debates.question: Does God command Isaiah to actively cause hardening in verses 9-10, or does He describe the certain result of the people's continued rejection?

views.label: A real, active hardening as judgment
views.case: The Hebrew imperatives, make the hearts of this people calloused, are genuinely commands, suggesting God actively judicially hardens a people who have already persistently rejected Him, similar to the pattern with Pharaoh in Exodus.

views.label: Idiomatic description of certain outcome
views.case: Hebrew sometimes uses imperative or causative forms to describe an outcome that will certainly follow from a course of action already chosen, so the command language describes what Isaiah's faithful preaching will, in effect, accomplish among a people already resistant to God.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the hardening is a real, terrible consequence following genuine, prior human rejection of God's word, not an arbitrary act preventing willing repentance; the New Testament's own use of this text, Acts 28:26-27, treats it as describing deserved judicial hardening.

study.originalLanguages: The seraphim, burning ones, veil their own faces before the thrice-repeated qadosh, holy, the strongest possible Hebrew superlative, applied to no other divine attribute this way in Scripture. Isaiah's avon, iniquity, is removed by the altar's burning coal, a cleansing that requires contact with sacrifice, and the surviving remnant is named the zera qadosh, the holy seed, an image of a stump that directly anticipates the shoot rising from the stump of Jesse in Isaiah 11:1.

typology.type: The burning coal from the altar cleansing Isaiah's unclean lips, Isaiah 6:6-7
typology.fulfillment: The full and final atonement accomplished through contact with Christ, the true altar and sacrifice, whose blood cleanses the conscience, Hebrews 9:14.

typology.type: The holy seed left as a stump, Isaiah 6:13
typology.fulfillment: The shoot that springs from the stump of Jesse, Christ Himself, rising from Israel's near-total devastation, Isaiah 11:1.

study.christ: John explicitly identifies the enthroned figure Isaiah saw, high and exalted, as Christ's own pre-incarnate glory, John 12:39-41, tying this vision directly to Jesus. Jesus later quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 word for word to explain why He speaks in parables to a hardened generation, Matthew 13:14-15, and the apostles apply the same hardening text to Israel's rejection of the risen Christ in Acts 28:26-27 and Romans 11:8, showing that the pattern begun in Isaiah's day continues in the response to the Messiah Himself.
study.god: God's holiness is so overwhelming that even sinless seraphim cover their faces in His presence, yet this same God graciously initiates the cleansing of a ruined sinner who could never have approached Him unaided. His justice can give a persistently rebellious people over to the hardness they have chosen, while His mercy simultaneously and always preserves a holy seed through whom His purposes continue.

crossReferences.note: Identifies the enthroned LORD Isaiah saw as Christ Himself in His pre-incarnate glory.
Jesus quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 directly to explain the purpose and effect of His parables on hardened hearers.
Paul applies this same hardening text to Israel's continued rejection of the gospel.
Draws on this same theme of a spirit of stupor within Paul's larger argument about Israel's partial, temporary hardening and God's ultimate mercy.

study.oneStory: Isaiah 6 shows a pattern that runs through the whole Bible: real encounter with the holy God exposes real human sin, yet that same God provides cleansing and calls the cleansed into costly mission, even when that mission meets hardened rejection. The holy seed surviving as a stump carries the story forward to Christ, the shoot who rises from apparent devastation to accomplish the atonement the altar's coal could only foreshadow.

sermon.title: Holy, Holy, Holy Is the Lord of Hosts
sermon.why: Piper works directly through Isaiah 6:1-8, drawing out the overwhelming holiness of God, Isaiah's ruin and cleansing, and the pattern of true calling that flows from a genuine vision of God's glory, exactly the movement this study traces.

quiz.q: What is significant about the seraphim covering their faces and feet even in God's presence?

options.text: It models the appropriate reverence and creaturely humility even sinless beings owe to the thrice-holy God.
options.note: If unfallen angels veil themselves before God's holiness, it heightens the weight of Isaiah's own unworthiness as a sinful man.

options.text: It shows the seraphim were ashamed of their own sin.
options.note: Seraphim are not depicted as sinful; their veiling expresses reverence, not guilt.

options.text: It is simply a description of their physical anatomy with no theological meaning.
options.note: The detail carries clear theological weight about appropriate creaturely response to divine holiness.

options.text: It shows they were hiding from an enemy.
options.note: The context is worship and reverence before God's throne, not fear of an external enemy.

quiz.q: Why does Isaiah cry, 'Woe is me, for I am ruined,' upon seeing the LORD?

options.text: The vision of God's absolute holiness exposed his own uncleanness and that of his people with unbearable clarity.
options.note: Genuine encounter with holiness produces genuine conviction of sin, the pattern seen elsewhere in Scripture.

options.text: He was physically injured by the smoke in the temple.
options.note: His ruin is moral and spiritual, explicitly tied to his unclean lips, not a physical injury.

options.text: He was afraid of losing his prophetic office.
options.note: His concern is his own sinfulness before a holy God, not job security.

options.text: He doubted whether the vision was real.
options.note: Isaiah does not question the vision's reality; he responds with immediate, genuine conviction of sin.

quiz.q: What does the burning coal from the altar accomplish in verse 7?

options.text: It removes Isaiah's iniquity and atones for his sin, cleansing him to be able to serve.
options.note: The cleansing comes through contact with the altar, foreshadowing atonement accomplished at the cross.

options.text: It grants Isaiah supernatural physical strength.
options.note: The stated effect is moral and spiritual cleansing, not physical enhancement.

options.text: It punishes Isaiah for his confession.
options.note: The coal brings cleansing and atonement, not punishment; Isaiah's confession is met with grace.

options.text: It silences Isaiah so he cannot speak further.
options.note: The coal touches his lips to cleanse them, not to silence him; he immediately speaks again in verse 8.

quiz.q: How should the hard commission in verses 9-10 be understood?

options.text: As describing the certain, deserved hardening that follows a people's persistent, prior rejection of God's word.
options.note: The New Testament's own use of this text, Acts 28:26-27, treats it as describing judicial hardening after sustained unbelief.

options.text: As proof that God arbitrarily prevents anyone from ever repenting.
options.note: This misreads the passage as removing all human responsibility, contrary to how the New Testament applies it.

options.text: As a command Isaiah refused to obey.
options.note: Isaiah accepts and carries out this commission; there is no indication of refusal.

options.text: As applying only to Isaiah's original eighth century audience, with no later significance.
options.note: The New Testament explicitly reapplies this text to responses to Jesus and the apostolic gospel, showing ongoing significance.

quiz.q: What does the closing image of the holy seed as a stump, verse 13, anticipate?

options.text: The shoot that will spring from the stump of Jesse in Isaiah 11:1, ultimately fulfilled in Christ.
options.note: The stump and shoot imagery directly links this remnant promise to the coming Messiah.

options.text: The complete and final destruction of Israel with no future hope.
options.note: A stump, unlike total destruction, implies the possibility of new growth, a deliberate note of hope.

options.text: A literal agricultural policy for replanting trees.
options.note: The stump is a symbolic image for a surviving remnant, not literal forestry instruction.

options.text: The restoration of the ten northern tribes specifically.
options.note: The holy seed and stump imagery is a broader remnant motif, developed messianically rather than tied to one specific tribal restoration.

questions: Isaiah's vision of God's holiness immediately exposed his own sinfulness. When was the last time you had a real, humbling encounter with who God actually is, rather than a comfortable idea of Him?
Isaiah did not clean himself up before God acted; the coal came to him. Where do you still try to make yourself presentable to God before letting Him cleanse you?
Isaiah's response to being cleansed was immediate availability: here am I, send me. What is God asking of you right now that you have been slow to volunteer for?
Isaiah was sent to a people who would not listen, yet he still went. Where do you need to remain faithful to obey God even when the response to your obedience may be resistance rather than success?

Even in devastating judgment, God preserved a holy seed. Where in your own life or circumstances do you need to trust that God is preserving something for future growth, even when everything visible looks like a stump?

translationNotes.note: Seraphim, saraph in the singular, means burning ones and appears elsewhere in the Old Testament describing fiery serpents, Numbers 21:6-8; here the term describes radiant, fiery heavenly attendants rather than serpentine beings.

The Hebrew imperatives, make the hearts of this people calloused, are debated between an active causative sense, God actively hardens, and a resultative sense describing what Isaiah's preaching will, in effect, produce among a people already resistant.

'Holy seed' is a striking phrase used elsewhere only in Ezra 9:2 for post-exilic Israel; here it names the small surviving remnant as itself sacred, set apart for God's ongoing purposes despite catastrophic judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 6:1-13.
